Name: gpu_inference_optimization
Title: Hardware-Faithful Optimization of Machine Learning Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By applying kernel-level throughput engineering and empirically tuned heuristics, we can significantly improve the performance of machine learning inference on commodity GPUs, achieving a 20% reduction in latency and a 15% increase in throughput.
Related Work: Existing research, such as AMP-ViT and SNIP, has focused on optimizing machine learning models using adaptive mixed-precision quantization and subbyte precision training. However, these methods often rely on theoretical bounds or simulations, rather than hardware-faithful evaluation. Our proposal builds upon the existing work by providing a comprehensive analysis of kernel-level optimizations and their impact on real-world performance.
Abstract: This proposal presents a novel approach to optimizing machine learning inference on commodity GPUs. By focusing on kernel-level throughput engineering and empirically tuned heuristics, we aim to improve the performance of machine learning models on commodity GPUs. Our approach involves optimizing fused CUDA/Triton kernels, memory-bandwidth utilization, and tensor-core occupancy. We will evaluate our approach using a range of machine learning models and datasets, providing a detailed analysis of the results and their implications for real-world applications.
Experiments: Optimizing fused CUDA/Triton kernels for machine learning inference using nvprof and Nsight tools
Evaluating the impact of memory-bandwidth utilization on performance using achieved bandwidth and arithmetic intensity metrics
Analyzing the effect of tensor-core occupancy on performance using occupancy and warp stall reasons metrics
Comparing our approach to existing optimization techniques, such as quantization and sparsity, using p50/p99 latency and throughput metrics
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of optimizing kernel-level throughput engineering
The need for specialized hardware and software expertise
The potential for limited generalizability to other hardware platforms
